calendar.matters:
Notice Of Application And Hearing For Right To Attach Order And For Order For Issuance Of Writ Of Attachment
Off Calendar Oct-07-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Michael Weisberg, Esq. of Brutzkus Gubner LLP, T:818.827.9000, for petitioner and moving party;
John R. Baum, Esq. and Sean Kramer, Esq. of Hirschfeld Kraemer LLP, T:415.835.9006, for defendants and opposing party.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of arguments, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on PETITIONER GREGORY WELCH's Application For Right To Attach Order And For Order For Issuance Of Writ Of Attachment, as follows:

Plaintiff's application for a writ of attachment is denied. A party to an arbitration agreement may file in superior court for a provisional remedy, including a writ of application. (Code Civ. Proc. 1281.8(b).) However, in such circumstances, the moving party must show "that the award to which the applicant may be entitled may be rendered ineffectual without provisional relief." (Id.) The "standards for irreparable harm set forth in [Code of Civil Procedure] section 485.010 provide guidance to the trial courts on the issue of ineffectual relief under section 1281.8." (California Retail Portfolio Fund GMBH & Co. KG v. Hopkins Real Estate Group (2011) 193 Cal.App.4th 849, 859.) Section 485.010 provides that a writ of attachment should only be issued if "great or irreparable injury would result to the plaintiff" and enumerates several ways of making that showing, including that "the defendant is insolvent in the sense that the defendant is generally not paying his or her debts as those debts become due, unless the debts are subject to a bona fide dispute." (Code Civ. Proc. 485.010(b)(2).) In general, "the apparent insolvency of a party to an arbitration agreement, or other evidence showing that the party [is][ experiencing severe financial difficulties," may be sufficient to satisfy the ineffectual relief requirement. (California Retail Portfolio Fund GMBH & Co. KG, 193 Cal.App.4th at 857.) Plaintiff contends that "[t]he rate of spending indicated by [Defendant] DVELE's own bank records demonstrates that DEVELE will likely run out of capital before the arbitration hearing." (Pl. Appl. at 8.) However, that contention is not supported by substantial evidence, as it is based on only three bank statements, the most recent of which is dated January 31, 2019, which provide no evidence as to Defendant's current financial condition. (Welch Decl. 11-12 & Ex. 4.) Predictions based on out-of-date financial statements or a cash-flow statement projecting a year-end loss are insufficient to meet Plaintiff's burden to show that defendant is likely to be "either insolvent or unable to pay money damages." (California Retail Portfolio Fund GMBH & Co. KG, 193 Cal.App.4th at 862.) Defendant's showing establishes that it is solvent, has met its debts and other financial obligations and has not defaulted on any such obligations, is financially sound, in good financial standing with its creditors, and has "millions of dollars" in its bank accounts. (Goodjohn Decl. 9.) Further, at no point has it declared or even considered declaring bankruptcy. (Id.)

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com
